coredata.dc:description: Solid-state multicolor carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention due to their wide applications. However, the fluorescence quenching of solid-state CDs occurs due to the π-π stacking or energy transfer between adjacent carbon dots. In addition, it is difficult to observe the afterglow of solid-state CDs due to their self-quenching and deliquescence. Here, electrochemically prepared multicolor CDs emit the solid-state fluorescence and afterglow emission with lifetimes up to 657 ms by embedding the CDs into urea matrix. More importantly, the surface amino groups and C = O bonds regulate the afterglow emission of CDs from phosphorescence and thermally activated delayed fluorescence at room temperature. Surface amino groups of multicolor CDs can enhance the spin–orbit coupling, increase the intersystem crossing (ISC) and reverse intersystem crossing (RISC). Thus it can promote the generation of triple excited states and regulate the singlet–triplet energy gap value from 0.384 to 0.026 eV. The formation of covalent bonds stabilizes the single and triple excited states and inhibits the non-radiative recombination, which achieves the enhanced fluorescence and visual afterglow of multicolor CDs. The fluorescence and afterglow CDs can be applied to dual signal imaging and anti-counterfeiting, which broadens the practical applications for solid-state CDs via surface state modulation.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical synthesis of multicolor carbon dots with room temperature phosphorescence to thermally activated delayed fluorescence via surface state modulation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-state multicolor carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention due to their wide applications. However, the fluorescence quenching of solid-state CDs occurs due to the π-π stacking or energy transfer between adjacent carbon dots. In addition, it is difficult to observe the afterglow of solid-state CDs due to their self-quenching and deliquescence. Here, electrochemically prepared multicolor CDs emit the solid-state fluorescence and afterglow emission with lifetimes up to 657 ms by embedding the CDs into urea matrix. More importantly, the surface amino groups and C = O bonds regulate the afterglow emission of CDs from phosphorescence and thermally activated delayed fluorescence at room temperature. Surface amino groups of multicolor CDs can enhance the spin–orbit coupling, increase the intersystem crossing (ISC) and reverse intersystem crossing (RISC). Thus it can promote the generation of triple excited states and regulate the singlet–triplet energy gap value from 0.384 to 0.026 eV. The formation of covalent bonds stabilizes the single and triple excited states and inhibits the non-radiative recombination, which achieves the enhanced fluorescence and visual afterglow of multicolor CDs. The fluorescence and afterglow CDs can be applied to dual signal imaging and anti-counterfeiting, which broadens the practical applications for solid-state CDs via surface state modulation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow materials have great potential applications in imaging, anti-counterfeiting and multi-level information encryption due to their long luminescence lifetime and high signal-to-noise ratio [1–3]. As a new type of afterglow nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) are mainly composed of carbon cores and surface groups, which are spherical particles with a size generally less than 10 nm [4]. CDs have attracted much attention due to their advantages such as adjustable band gap, low toxicity, easy preparation and modification [5]. Liquid CDs are not directly used in solid-state applications such as solid-state imaging, anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption [6,7]. This is mainly due to the excellent luminescence properties of most CDs in water or solvent dispersed states, which can not be transferred to the solid state due to the serious luminescence quenching during the conversion process [8]. Therefore, solid state CDs are expected to be used in the fields of imaging, anti-counterfeiting and information security [9]. However, aggregation-induced quenching luminescence occurs in the solid state CDs, which limits the application of CDs in the field of solid-state luminescence [10,11].

It is found that the aggregation-induced quenching luminescence of the CDs is mainly induced by the resonance energy transfer or π-π interaction [12]. At present, the matrix composite strategy is one of the effective strategies to prevent the solid-state quenching luminescence. Composite matrix materials can be divided into polymer matrices, inorganic matrices (silica, BaSO4, NaCl, and ZrO2), and layered matrices (layered double hydroxides) [13]. Moreover, the CDs can realize afterglow luminescence through the matrix compounding strategy due to the solidification of triplet excitons by the matrix [14,15]. Afterglow luminescence contains room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF). At present, most of the reported carbon-based afterglow materials are in a single RTP luminescence mode due to the stabilization of triplet excitons by the matrix [16]. The coexistence of RTP and TADF process requires a strict energy gap between singlet and triplet states, as well as a stable exciton emission state [17]. Recently, it is still observed the afterglow caused by the co-emission of RTP and TADF in CDs-based materials via special strategies. Lu et al. introduced cross-linking force between the original CDs and the urea matrix to increase the degree of conjugation and the surface carboxyl functional groups, which achieved RTP and TADF dual-emission afterglow of CDs [18]. Hu et al. used multiple ionic bonds to form rigid networks and inhibit the non-radiative recombination of triplet excited states, thus realizing the thermally enhanced TADF emission of CDs [19]. Liu et al. regulated the RTP emission of CDs into TADF emission by increasing the temperature with the improvement of molecular vibration and non-radiative decay [20]. Some special strategies have been developed to achieve TADF emission of CDs-based materials. However, the TADF emission of CDs-based materials can only be observed by heating at present, because the room temperature TADF requires a very small value of ΔEST. Therefore, it is urgent to develop new strategies to achieve single-mode afterglow of long-wavelength TADF for CDs at room temperature.

Thermally activated delayed fluorescence is generated by triplet excitons transitioning to singlet vibrational energy levels through reverse intersystem crossing under external thermal stimulation [21]. It is necessary to have a sufficiently small value of singlet–triplet energy gap to achieve TADF [22]. Some strategies have been developed to regulate the singlet–triplet energy gap of CDs. Yu et al used (4-(2-aminoethyl)-morpholine and 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine as organic templates to regulate the ΔEST values from 0.36 to 0.18 eV, thus realizing green RTP and blue TADF of CDs [23]. In addition, Jeon et al. adopted chemical oxidation strategy to increase the oxidation degree of GQDs (γOC increases from 4.63 % to 59.6 %) with corresponding value of ΔEST from 0.365 to 0.123 eV, which led to the blue RTP and green TADF emission [24]. However, the value of ΔEST is not small enough to achieve TADF emission at room temperature [22]. Therefore, it is still a great challenge to realize the full-color CDs from RTP to room temperature TADF by modulating the energy gap via the surface state strategy.

As shown in Table S1, the CDs prepared by electrochemical method have fewer surface groups and are easy to be surface modified, which is beneficial to investigate the effects of surface states on luminescence mechanism. At present, most of the electrochemically synthesized CDs possess fluorescence properties, but no afterglow luminescence has been observed [25–27]. In this work, we achieve solid-state fluorescence enhancement and afterglow luminescence by combining urea matrices with electrochemically synthesized multicolor CDs (blue, green and red emitting CDs for B-CDs, G-CDs, R-CDs). After recombination, the quantum yields of solid-state B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea are improved to 3.42 %, 3.06 % and 15.01 % respectively. More important, B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea, and R-CDs/Urea can emit yellowish green, orange and red afterglow luminescence, respectively. The surface amino groups of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea can regulate the energy gap between the lowest triplet state (T1) and singlet state (S1) from 0.384 to 0.026 eV, which realizes the RTP to TADF emission at room temperature. Based on the excellent afterglow and solid-state luminescence properties of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea, multicolor CDs are successfully applied to the solid-state imaging, information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. This work reports a surface state regulation strategy to realize the regulation of RTP and TADF of CDs composites by embedding CDs with surface amino groups into urea matrix. This research provides a new insight to understanding the afterglow luminescence mechanism of CDs. And it also opens a new direction for electrochemically synthesized CDs to afterglow materials in the field of solid-state imaging, advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting and multi-level information encryption.

Graphite rods (Φ 6 mm) were purchased from Tianjin Zhongnuo Carbon Co., Ltd. 1, 8-diaminonaphthalene was acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. O-phenylenediamine was bought from Tianjin Jinke Fine Chemical Research Institute. P-phenylenediamine and urea were obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. All chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade, commercially available and directly used as received without further purification. Deionized water (18 MΩ·cm−1) was utilized throughout all experiments in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea, R-CDs/Urea powder
ce:section.ce:para: The multicolor CDs (M−CDs) with surface modification were prepared by an electrochemical method according to our previous work [28]. In detail, the O-phenylenediamine and P-phenylenediamine as surface modification additives were firstly dissolved in deionized water at room temperature. Subsequently, two graphite rods were dipped in the above electrolyte under 60 V for 3 h. Finally, dark red solution was obtained as R-CDs precursor solution for further use. Besides, B-CDs and G-CDs precursor solution were synthesized using 1,8-diaminonaphthalene and O-phenylenediamine as surface modification additives using the similar method, respectively.

Solid R-CDs/Urea composites were synthesized by a modified method [29]. Typically, the urea (8 g) was dispersed into 15 mL of R-CDs solution to form a uniform solution. Afterwards, the homogeneous solution was heated in a drying oven under 155 °C for 5 h. Finally, the dark red bulk products were ground into powder to obtain solid R-CDs/Urea composites. The detailed synthesis process of B-CDs/Urea and G-CDs/Urea was similar with that of R-CDs/Urea.

In order to investigate the morphologies of the CDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the samples were obtained by a Talos F200S with acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The steady-state fluorescence spectra, absolute quantum yields (QY) and fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, FL-3–22) at room temperature. The fluorescence spectrophotometer is equipped with a Xenon lamp as the excitation source and an integrating sphere. The afterglow spectra of the CDs were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, FL-1039/40). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured with a spectrophotometer (Hitachi, U-3900H). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the CDs were recorded from 500 to 4000 cm−1 on a Bruker WQF-410 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of the CDs were carried out using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI with monochromatic Al Kα as the excitation source. Raman spectra were recorded on a laser confocal Raman micro-spectroscope (LabRam HR Evolution) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray power diffraction (XRD) measurements of the CDs were carried out using a Bruker D8 Discover with Cu Kα radiation.

The solid-state luminescent M−CDs/Urea composites were synthesized by confining CDs with bule, green and red fluorescence in urea matrix, as displayed in Fig. 1
a and (Fig. S1a-b). Herein, M−CDs were synthesized by an electrochemical method [28]. As shown in Fig. 1 b and Fig. S2a-l, the M−CDs/Urea composites exhibit bright fluorescence after the incorporation of M−CDs into urea under the irradiation of UV-light (365 nm), but neither the solid M−CDs nor the urea powder could emit fluorescence (Fig. S3a-d). More surprisingly, M−CDs/Urea composites display bright multicolor afterglow at room temperature (Fig. 1 b and Fig. S2j-l). In order to obtain an optimum fluorescence performance of M−CDs/Urea composites, different temperatures and various contents of urea on fluorescence for M−CDs/Urea composites were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4a-c, S5a-c and S6a-c, when the urea amount was used with 8 g, 8 g and 8 g, B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites exhibit the better photoluminescence performance, respectively. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images show that B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs are uniformly distributed, and the average particle sizes are 2.75 nm, 2.94 nm and 2.84 nm, respectively (Fig. S7a-c). As shown in HRTEM images (Fig. S7d-e), the lattice spacing of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs is about 0.21 nm. After the simple heating treatment, B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs are successfully embedded in urea matrix, which can be detected in Fig. 2
a-c. The average diameter of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs embedded in urea matrix is 2.83 nm, 3.09 nm and 2.82 nm, respectively. The HRTEM images displayed in Fig. 2 d-f indicate that B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs embedded in urea matrix also possess the same lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with that of graphite (100) planes [30]. In addition, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs show a broad peak at ∼ 25°, which is the characteristic peak of amorphous carbon (Fig. S8a) [31]. The M−CDs/Urea composites possess similar peaks to that of heat-treated urea, indicating that M−CDs are uniformly embedded in the urea matrix (Fig. S8b) [18].

The fluorescence and afterglow properties of M−CDs/Urea composites are investigated, as displayed in Fig. 3
a-b. B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites emit optimal bluish purple, green and red fluorescence centered at 420 nm, 538 nm and 618 nm under the excitation of 365 nm, 420 nm and 460 nm, respectively. Furthermore, B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites exhibit independent excitation fluorescence emission with optimal excitation of 370 nm, 400 nm and 550 nm (Fig. S9a-d), respectively. Compared with the emission and excitation spectra of M−CDs solution, fluorescence emission of M−CDs/Urea composites are still located at blue, green and red region, as shown in Fig. S10a-d. The results indicate that the multicolor fluorescence of the M−CDs/Urea composites and M−CDs is originated from the same luminescence center with the surface amino groups, which is derived from our previous research [28]. Since the M−CDs/Urea composites showed bright multicolor afterglow at room temperature, we measured the afterglow spectra of the samples, as shown in Fig. 3 b and Fig. S11a-c. Interestingly, the shift of the afterglow peak and the fluorescence peak of the three M−CDs/Urea composites is different. B-CDs/Urea composite shows a single afterglow emission at 545 nm, which is shifted 125 nm compared to the fluorescence peak. An obvious red shift exists between the peaks of fluorescence and afterglow, indicating that the main component of the afterglow is room temperature phosphorescence [20]. G-CDs/Urea composite has three afterglow emission peaks at 538 nm, 564 nm and 666 nm. The afterglow emission peak at 538 nm is consistent with the fluorescence emission peak, and the other two afterglow peaks show obvious red shift relative to the fluorescence peak. It indicates that the afterglow emission is partly derived from TADF and partly from RTP [32].The afterglow emission peak of R-CDs/Urea composite is consistent with the fluorescence emission peak at 618 nm, indicating that the afterglow is derived from TADF [24]. As shown in Fig. S12a, the afterglow intensity of B-CDs/Urea composites decreases significantly with the increase of temperature, which proves that the afterglow of CDs is induced by the RTP [18]. Within a certain temperature range from 127 to 297 K, a gradual increase of afterglow emission intensity of G-CDs/Urea composites at 538 nm, and a decrease tendency of the other two afterglow emission peaks can be observed (Fig.S12b). It indicates that the afterglow emission peak at 538 nm is the characteristic of TADF, and the afterglow emission peaks at 635 and 556 nm are the characteristics of RTP [19,20]. As shown in Fig. S12c, at 127 ∼ 297 K, the intensity of R-CDs/Urea composite afterglow emission increases with the increase of temperature, which proves that the afterglow emission of R-CDs/Urea is originated from TADF [21]. In addition, the afterglow emissions of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites have a blue shift at lower temperatures, which may be caused by Locally-excited state [33]. The solid M−CDs/Urea composites greatly avoid the solid-state fluorescence quenching, and the fluorescence intensity is improved compared to M−CDs solution. Surprisingly, after R-CDs are embedded in the urea matrix, the absolute QY of R-CDs/Urea (15.01 %) powder is significantly enhanced compared with that of R-CDs (0.44 %) solution, as shown in the Table S2. In addition, the QY of B-CDs/Urea (3.42 %) and G-CDs/Urea (3.06 %) is also enhanced compared with that of B-CDs (3.30 %) and G-CDs (1.35 %) solution. The difference of fluorescence enhancement for M−CDs/Urea composites is induced by the electron radiation and non-radiative recombination originating from the different states on M−CDs/Urea composites [34,35]. The electron relaxation is investigated by time-resolved fluorescence decay curves to clarify the fluorescence enhancement mechanism in solid M−CDs/Urea composites. As shown in Fig. 3 c, Fig. S13 and Table S3, the average fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites are prolonged by 17.32, 12.00 and 36.11 ns relative to B-CDs (7.37 ns), G-CDs (3.19 ns) and R-CDs (1.81 ns), respectively. The decrease of non-radiative recombination leads to the increase of fluorescence lifetime, which indicates that the solid-state recombination of M−CDs could suppress non-radiative recombination. The inhibition of non-radiative recombination is due to the severe limitation of rotational relaxation of phenyl molecules on the surface of M−CDs/Urea composites [36,37]. As shown in Table S4, the radiative rate kR of R-CDs/Urea (4.16 × 106 s−1) is obviously higher than that of B-CDs/Urea (1.97 × 106 s−1) and G-CDs/Urea (2.55 × 106 s−1), and the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs/Urea is also significantly enhanced. More importantly, the afterglow decay spectra of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites are shown in Fig. 3 d. The average lifetimes of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites are calculated to be 657, 166 and 64 ms, indicating the long-life afterglow emission characteristics of the three solid CDs/Urea composites at room temperature (Table S5). As shown in Fig. S14a-c, the afterglow and fluorescence spectra of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea, and R-CDs/Urea composites are also measured at 77 K. The value of energy gap (ΔEST) for B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea, and R-CDs/Urea composites was calculated to be 0.384 eV (S1 = 1240/430 eV, T1 = 1240/496 eV), 0.147 eV (S1 = 1240/486 eV, T1 = 1240/516 eV) and 0.026 eV (S1 = 1240/606 eV and T1 = 1240/614 eV).The ΔEST of M−CDs/Urea composites contributes to the ISC and RISC process, thereby achieving RTP and TADF emission [23,24]. As shown in Fig. 3e, the UV–Vis absorption spectra are investigated to explore the solid-state luminescence origin of M−CDs/Urea composites. In the spectral range below 300 nm, three absorption peaks of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites centered at 251 nm, 278 nm and 280 nm are corresponded to the π–π* transition of C = C bonds for the M−CDs/Urea composites. In addition, M−CDs/Urea composites possess broad absorption bands which is attributed to n–π* transition of surface functional groups (C-N/C-O bonds) in the long wavelength region (above 300 nm) [31,38,39]. As displayed in the Fig. 3e and Fig. S9d, the broad absorption bands of M−CDs/Urea composites centered at 300–600 nm and the excitation spectra of M−CDs/Urea composites present a good overlap. It indicates that surface groups of M−CDs/Urea composites are contributed to the solid-state luminescence of M−CDs/Urea composites in the blue, green and red region [40]. In addition, the mechanism of tunable RTP and TADF of M−CDs/Urea composites also should be explored.

The multicolor luminescence of M−CDs/Urea composites and M−CDs is originated from the same luminescent centers, which is mainly affected by the surface states. After compounding, the surface functional groups on M−CDs/Urea composites were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. As shown in the Fig. 3 f, B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites possess similar absorption peaks of O-H and N-H at around 3450 and 3346 cm−1. In addition, the characteristic peaks appear at 3031, 1658, 1470, 1163 and 1062 cm−1 are corresponded to the C-H, C = O, N-H, C-N and C-O bonds, respectively [13,41–43]. Based on the above results, B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites have similar surface functional groups including oxygen and nitrogen-related groups (carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, carbonyl groups, amino groups, etc). From the FTIR spectra of M−CDs (Fig. S15), it indicates that M−CDs/Urea composites possess similar groups to M−CDs. In addition, the Raman spectra reveal that the ID/IG ratio of B-CDs/Urea (0.95), G-CDs/Urea (0.94) and R-CDs/Urea (0.93) is close to that of B-CDs (0.95), G-CDs (0.93) and R-CDs (0.92), as shown in Fig. S16a-b (D peak: 1395 cm−1, G peak: 1596 cm−1) [44]. It shows that the disorder degree of M−CDs is not enhanced after recombination, which suggests that M−CDs/urea composites do not form more defects or structural distortion. According to our previous studies, the multicolor luminescence properties of M−CDs/Urea composites and M−CDs are derived from the surface amino groups [28].

The composition and related chemical bonding states of surface functional groups for M−CDs/Urea composites and M−CDs are further confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. As shown in Fig. 4
a and Fig. S17a, M−CDs/Urea composites and M−CDs present the signals of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, which confirms the presence of C, N, and O elements at the surface of M−CDs/Urea composites and M−CDs [12,45,46]. The atomic contents of C, N, and O elements for M−CDs/Urea composites are shown in Table S6. The proportion of C, N, and O elements is 38.46 %, 36.87 %, and 24.67 % for B-CDs/Urea, 40.46 %, 34.37 %, and 25.17 % for G-CDs/Urea, and 34.50 %, 38.25 %, 27.25 % for R-CDs/Urea. It suggests that N elements are gradually increased for G-CDs/Urea, B-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites. In order to further investigate the bond configuration of C, O and N atoms in the M−CDs/Urea composites, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s and N 1 s are investigated. As shown in the C 1 s and O 1 s spectra (Fig. 4 b-c), the C elements exist as C = C/C-C (ca. 284.0 eV), C-N (ca. 285.0 eV), C-O (ca. 287.0 eV), and C = O bonds (ca. 288.0 eV) for the M−CDs/Urea composites. And the O elements appear in the form of C-O (ca. 532.0 eV) and C = O bonds (ca. 535.0 eV) [40,47,48]. In addition, the contents of C-N bonds in R-CDs/Urea composites (30.65 %) are higher than that in B-CDs/Urea (25.07 %) and G-CDs/Urea composites (21.59 %) (Table S7). The result is consistent with the higher QY of the R-CDs/Urea (15.01 %) relative to B-CDs/Urea (3.42 %) and G-CDs/Urea (3.06 %) composites (Table S2). The high-resolution N 1 s spectra (Fig. 4 d) show that the N elements exist in the form of amino groups (ca. 399.0 eV) for the M−CDs/Urea composites [49,50]. The XPS results are basically consistent with the FTIR analysis, and the surface of M−CDs/Urea composites is covered with O and N-related groups such as carbonyl, hydroxyl and amino groups. Moreover, the C-N bonds play a significant role in affecting the QY of M−CDs/Urea composites. The XPS results disclose that M−CDs and M−CDs/Urea composites have the same surface elements and chemical bonding states, while the contents of related elements and chemical bonds are different, as shown in Fig. S17a-d. The atomic contents of C (78.01 %, 71.84 %, and 75.20 %), O (7.13 %, 11.60 % and 7.03 %), and N (14.86 %,16.55 % and 17.77 %) elements for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs are calculated and shown in Table S8, which reveals that N elements are gradually increased. For the C 1 s spectra, the contents of C = O bonds (8.78 %, 12.48 % and 14.70 % for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs) are increased gradually (Fig. S17b and Table S9). The contents of O = C bonds (56.37 %, 67.88 %, and 86.12 % for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs) are also increased obviously in the O 1 s spectra (Fig. S17c and Table S10). The N 1 s spectra (Fig. S17d) reveal that the nitrogen element could be attributed to the amino groups (ca. 399.0 eV) in the M−CDs, and the relative contents of amino groups are increased gradually for the B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs. The increase of surface amino groups affects the surface luminescence centers of M−CDs [28]. In addition, the C = O bonds possess a strong spin coupling effect, which promotes the intersystem crossing process between singlet and triplet states, and contributes to the generation of triplet states [24]. Therefore, the tunable RTP and TADF emission of M−CDs/Urea composites are related to the increase of surface amino groups and C = O bonds.

Based on the above results (FTIR, XPS and Raman)，surface bonding configuration of M−CDs/Urea composites is shown in Fig. 5
a. The surface of M−CDs contains amino and oxygen-containing groups (carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups). After combination with urea, M−CDs are bonded to urea through covalent bonds of C-N bonds and confined to the urea matrix. Notably, M−CDs/Urea composites display solid-state enhancement fluorescence without fluorescence band shift relative to solution-state M−CDs. The surface amino groups are still the luminescent centers of M−CDs/Urea composites, which is consistent with the M−CDs before recombination. Based on our previous studies, the increase of amino groups can lead to the narrowing of the band gap and the red shift of emission [28]. As an electron-donating group, the amino groups can effectively increase the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) electron orbitals, thus reducing the band gap [51]. The ΔEST of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea, and R-CDs/Urea is 0.384 eV, 0.147 eV and 0.026 eV, respectively (Fig. S14a-c). Combining with spectral analysis, the possible energy level structures of M−CDs/Urea composites is displayed in Fig. 5 b. Under radiation, the electrons on surface groups (mostly NH2) are excited from the HOMO to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The urea matrix stabilizes the triplet state of M−CDs/Urea by covalent bonds. After that, the excited electrons in different energy states of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites transfer to the ground state, thus producing bluish purple, green and red fluorescence, respectively. Besides, the excited electrons transfer energy in the form of non-radiative recombination through vibrational relaxation. The fast free rotation between non-rigid fluorescent chromophores is one of the important ways to dissipate excitation energy. After removing the irradiation, M−CDs/Urea composites exhibit different afterglow emissions. B-CDs/Urea composites emit yellowish green RTP (545 nm). G-CDs/Urea composites emit green TADF (538 nm) and orange RTP (564 and 666 nm). R-CDs/Urea composites emit red TADF emission at room temperature (618 nm).

The fluorescence QY of R-CDs/Urea greatly exceeds that of solution-state R-CDs. Owing to the bonding between the urea matrix and R-CDs after curing, the intramolecular relaxation rotation is blocked, and the non-radiative dissipation pathway of excited state energy is inhibited. It has been proved that the molecular rotational relaxation as the luminescent center is strictly limited, which will weaken the energy dissipation of the excited state, thus prolonging the lifetimes of the luminescent materials. The fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs/Urea (36.11 ns) is significantly increased compared to R-CDs (1.81 ns), indicating that the non-radiative recombination is reduced and the rotational relaxation of surface molecules has been inhibited for the R-CDs/Urea. According to the XPS and FTIR results, it can be found that the contents of C-N bonds on the surface of R-CDs/Urea (30.65 %) are higher than that of B-CDs/Urea (25.07 %) and G-CDs/Urea (21.59 %). It indicates that the higher contents of C-N bonds are more closely connected to the urea matrix, resulting in strict restriction for the rotation of the surface molecules, so that the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs is significantly enhanced. In addition, the surface oxidation degree (relative contents of C-O and C = O) of R-CDs/Urea (47.79 %) is lower than that of B-CDs/Urea (51.08 %) and G-CDs/Urea (57.10 %), disclosing that the surface oxygen groups may act as reactive sites for binding to the urea matrix. Therefore, the surface groups and the urea matrix limit the rotational relaxation of the surface molecules as the luminescent center through the C-N bonds for solid M−CDs/Urea composites. The restriction between urea matrix and M−CDs can reduce the non-radiative energy dissipation of the excited state for the M−CDs/Urea composites and improve fluorescence quantum yields [52].

Moreover, the covalent bond effect between the urea matrix and M−CDs stabilizes the triplet state and avoids the quenching of the triplet state by external conditions such as oxygen. No afterglow emission is observed when M−CDs are physically mixed with urea at room temperature (Fig. S18a-f). The FTIR spectra of M−CDs/Urea composites after physical mixing at room temperature show that the intensity of the C-N (1163 cm−1) and C-O bonds (1062 cm−1) is decreased, respectively (Fig. S19). XPS results suggest that the contents of surface elements and bonding states of M−CDs/Urea treated by physical mixing and heating have been changed ( Fig. 4 a-d, Fig. S20a-d, Tables S6 and S11). The contents of C-N bonds in B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites treated by physical mixing are 22.07 %, 14.39 % and 24.96 %, respectively (Tables S12). It is lower than that of B-CDs/Urea (25.07 %), G-CDs/Urea (21.59 %) and R-CDs/Urea (30.65 %) composites treated by heating (Tables S7). The amidation between the carboxyl groups on the surface of M−CDs and the amino groups of urea leads to the increased contents of C-N bonds in M−CDs/Urea composites [18]. The contents of C-O bonds for B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites treated by physical mixing are 20.81 %, 30.74 % and 22.55 %, respectively (Tables S7). It is also lower than that of B-CDs/Urea (26.71 %), G-CDs/Urea (31.04 %) and R-CDs/Urea (24.26 %) composites treated by heating (Table S12). The decomposition of urea at higher temperature will produce cyanic acid, which leads to a slight increase of C-O contents [53]. Consequently, B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites display afterglow luminescence due to the stabilizing effect of covalent bonds.

In addition, the luminescent properties of M−CDs/Urea composites with tunable RTP and TADF emissions are related to the increased contents of amino groups and C = O bonds. It is found that the nitrogen-related groups possess the effect of promoting electron spin coupling, and the enhancement of spin coupling contributes to the generation of triplet states [32,54,55]. With the enhanced contents of amino groups, the spin coupling effect is further enhanced and ΔEST is further narrowed. Specifically, the ΔEST value of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea, and R-CDs/Urea is 0.384 eV, 0.147 eV and 0.026 eV, respectively. Since ΔEST of B-CDs/Urea is 0.384 eV, and the intersystem crossing is more likely to occur, resulting in room temperature phosphorescence emission. With the decreased ΔEST of G-CDs/Urea (0.147 eV), the intersystem crossing and reverse intersystem crossing is more likely to occur simultaneously, which results in RTP and TADF emissions. For the smallest ΔEST of R-CDs/Urea (0.026 eV), the intersystem crossing becomes dominant, which leads to the occurrence of TADF emission at room temperature.

The proliferation of counterfeit goods has become a concern due to the serious threat to consumers, brands and the economy [56]. Trademark is the key to identify the real or fake goods, and pattern anti-counterfeiting technology is a powerful tool to verify the authenticity of trademarks [57,58]. The realization of pattern anti-counterfeiting technology is mainly through the use of patterns containing microscopic features and optical effects on products or packaging [59–62]. Therefore, it is still a great challenge to develop low-toxic, metal-free and cheap luminescent materials with multi-dimensional signals. Since B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites possess a series of advantages such as bright solid-state fluorescence, room temperature afterglow luminescence, easy preparation and low cost. Therefore, we select M−CDs/Urea composites as phosphors to achieve anti-counterfeiting and encryption applications. The three M−CDs/Urea composites can be used as multi-level information encryption due to the different afterglow emission times. Furthermore, the M−CDs/Urea composites possess multicolor fluorescence, RTP, and TADF triple emission modes. Based on the above characteristics, M−CDs/Urea composites are used to fabricate a series of patterns with different anti-counterfeiting modes consisting of color-encoded fluorescence, color-encoded afterglow emission, and life-encoded afterglow emission modes. Firstly, the first-level protection mode of multi-color fluorescence and multi-color afterglow conversion is turned on. As shown in Fig. 6
a, when irradiated by 365 nm UV light, different patterns prepared by B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites exhibit bluish purple, green and red colors, respectively. As the UV lamp is turned off within 0.5 s, the three patterns show yellowish green, orange and red colors, respectively. Secondly, the sublevel protection mode of life-encoded afterglow emission is turned on. When the UV lamp is turned off within 1 to 2 s, only two patterns with green and orange colors can be observed. Furthermore, the three- level protection mode encoded by the afterglow color conversion is turned on. As time goes to 4 s, the pattern color prepared by G-CDs/Urea changes from orange to yellow, and the pattern color prepared by B-CDs/Urea is still yellowish green. Finally, the multicolor-coded fluorescence and multicolor-coded afterglow emission modes are turned off, and only the life-encoded afterglow emission mode is turned on, which forms a four-level protection mode. While the UV lamp is turned off for 5 s, only the pattern prepared by B-CDs/Urea composite can be observed. The results disclose that applications of M−CDs/Urea composite can transmit encrypted pattern information with changing over time. The characteristics of multi-color fluorescence and afterglow of CDs have achieved four-level anti-counterfeiting, which has great potential application in the field of advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting. In addition, The M−CDs/Urea composites can be applied to various forms of information encryption. As shown in Fig. 6 b, a flower pattern is drawn using two CDs powders, in which the petals are composed of G-CDs/Urea composite and R-CDs/Urea composite. When irradiated by UV light, the pattern is a red flower with green leaves. As the UV light is turned off, the color of the leaves changes from green to red. After a period of time, the patterns disappear in the field of vision. The orange pattern composed of B-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites presents a mixed color of bluish purple and red under UV light irradiation. As the UV lamp is off, the color of the orange becomes yellowish green and red, and then the pattern disappears. These results demonstrate that M−CDs/Urea composites have great potential applications in anti-counterfeiting technology and information encryption due to its different fluorescence and afterglow properties.

Multicolor solid-state enhanced fluorescence and room temperature afterglow were achieved by regulating the surface state of electrochemically synthesized CDs by urea matrix composite strategy. The fluorescence quantum yields of solid B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea, and R-CDs/Urea are 3.42 %, 3.06 % and 15.01 %, respectively. Moreover, the combination of urea and M−CDs mainly affects the contents of C-N bonds on the surface of M−CDs/Urea composites. The single bond rotation of the surface molecules is inhibited by the covalent bonding (C-N) of urea and CDs, and the transfer process of surface state energy is regulated with improving fluorescence quantum yields. In addition, the yellowish green afterglow of B-CDs/Urea composite is originated from RTP emission, the orange afterglow of G-CDs/Urea is derived from RTP and TADF emission, and the red afterglow of R-CDs/Urea is induced by TADF emission at room temperature. The afterglow lifetimes of solid B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea, and R-CDs/Urea are 657, 166 and 64 ms, respectively. The increased contents of amino groups and C = O bonds of B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea can regulate the value of ΔEST from 0.384 to 0.026 eV, which contributes to ISC and RISC, thus realizing tunable RTP to TADF emission at room temperature. Finally, B-CDs/Urea, G-CDs/Urea and R-CDs/Urea composites have great potential applications in solid-state imaging and information encryption.
